sourceMovie.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth for the same reason it broke through in 1997: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Hideaki Anno keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth clicked for you, Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion (1997) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and drama, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth?
faq.answer: Try Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion, Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone, Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance—each shares DNA with Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth?
faq.answer: Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth (1997) is best described as Animation, Drama, Science Fiction. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Neon Genesis Evangelion Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
